dd782c5489fc:
Exploration: scouts are sent out and left to it. Each claims a fixed wedge
around the home base and heads for the nearest unexplored frontier inside it,
widening only once its own wedge is done. The phase offset per scout is a golden
angle, so several scouts split the map instead of piling onto the same tile.
The wedge deliberately does not rotate with time: it used to, and a rotating
cone made scouts wander the map in zigzags while overlapping cones sent them all
to the same place.
97ef18b67179:
Fallback exploration targets: circle known enemy buildings at a safe distance to
keep the picture fresh, and revisit whichever explored tile has gone longest
without an update so an enemy cannot vanish by hiding in one. Together they mean
scouting never stops, even on a fully explored map.
3416fcefb97e: The nearest enemy defensive building this side has seen -- its threat ring.
18c3b0838560:
If the next step would enter the danger ring, stop at the nearest safe point
rather than keep trying: otherwise the scout oscillates on the boundary.
eb6cbb9a991c:
Waypoints, derived from map size and the enemy town centre so they work on every
map: north and south routes go around the lake, the centre route crosses via the
island, so bridges and fords actually carry traffic.
e467d016e808:
============ 9b0. Demand scoring: one plan pool, a commitment set, multi-resource
reservations and income allocation ============
This replaced four separate brains with a fixed priority order and four
generations of ad-hoc savings patches.
Each economy or strategy tick, every domain throws candidates into the pool with
a score (domain weight x urgency, computed only from facts about the game).
Candidates pass a gate in score order into the commitment set: cheap ones reserve
their full cost immediately, expensive ones open a savings bucket fed by income.
Once affordable they execute and leave the set.
The hard-won rules behind it: reservations bind at the spending end, so
non-committed spending cannot dip into them; a reservation must correspond to
something that will really be built; and there can only ever be one savings
bucket at a time, or every surplus is swallowed by the next bucket forever.
ca50a032e6e7:
Execution follows score rank: only a higher-scoring item that has not executed
yet can block you, so a low-priority reservation can never hold up a
high-priority one. Opportunistic items join the same ranking -- done if
affordable, reserving nothing if not. At most one building is placed per tick,
which keeps the AI's build-out paced rather than bursty.
aea5bac0c4d6:
============ 9b. AI economy: a player that follows the rules ============
It acts only through the same command whitelist the player has: train villagers,
shift the gathering split, add farms and coppices, put up markets, barracks and
monasteries, research, advance an age, trade at the market, expand with outposts.
Affordability, placement, unlock, age and civilisation-exclusivity gates apply to
it exactly as they do to the player, and gathering, building and fleeing run
through the same villager code -- including a symmetric danger map.
Economy decisions tick about every 2s, strategy about every 10s, and every random
draw comes from the seeded generator, so the same seed replays the same game.
4c47f595a52c:
Map feature extraction: the single source for villager targets, tower counts and
outpost caps.
Gatherable amount inside the economic radius gives the number of gatherers the
land can support; buildable free tiles give the cap on renewable sources. The
earlier version scaled those caps by villager count, which was circular --
villager target raised the farm cap, which raised the villager target.
5c2b9cb21a48:
The calibration constants live here rather than in the difficulty table, because
difficulty is not allowed to state quantities of buildings. A castle, for
instance, is gated on how much natural stone its economic radius can actually
supply -- far more than its bare price, since towers and walls draw on the same
stone.
8d94a8380972: 1. Gatherable amount inside the radius, plus ore outside it, in one pass.
2a4b818cd4cc:
3. Ore clusters outside the radius, merged greedily, each cluster's centre
following its members: how many places are worth expanding to.
9a589da0fcfa:
4. Points worth defending: the home approach, resource areas actually being
worked, and outposts. An area also qualifies after being raided twice -- during a
raid the villagers have already fled, so "is anyone working here" is false at
exactly the moment a tower is most needed.
7783a5d18774:
Castles are capped by the stone economy and the number of defensible points, not
by a number in the difficulty table. The difficulty tier only says how ambitious
to be: never, capital only, or capital plus anything worth permanently holding.
8fc9720122e6:
Timing is gated on the building's own age requirement plus an economic
threshold and having already survived a first attack -- not on the name of an
age. The old code required a further age beyond the unlock, which in practice
meant almost never: that extra gate, not a missing wire, is why the castle logic
looked dead for so long.
5d84f3075913:
Shared predicate: the fortification logic (should we build one) and the market
(should we stockpile stone for one) must use the same test, or the market
stockpiles stone for a castle that is not yet allowed to exist.
d57600f825f0:
Towers have three reactive triggers -- the home rally point, a resource area
raided twice, and a new outpost. No trigger means no tower is wanted right now.
This is shared rather than duplicated because the savings logic once only checked
"towers < cap" without asking whether there was anywhere to put one: it reserved
stone for a tower that could never be built, villagers were permanently diverted
to quarrying, and age advancement stalled for the whole game.
cc99ae71baea:
Four generations of ad-hoc savings patches -- for docks, siege workshops,
coppices and towers -- were retired into the commitment set. The same root cause
had been patched four times. Reservations are now one multi-resource vector, and
nothing outside the pool can spend into them.
ad40086db357:
1. Train villagers: a target per age, adjusted by difficulty and opening
template (rush fewer, boom more), with a self-limiting queue.
d4228314a660:
2. Gathering split follows the shortfall: the target stock is the baseline plus
the unpaid cost of everything committed, so villagers lean towards whatever is
missing.
3c0efda13b6a:
3. Farms and coppices for sustainability: when a natural resource inside the
radius runs out, build a renewable source -- villagers walk over and build it,
under the same rules as the player's.
2f9d8d383e1d:
The cap on farms and coppices is what the map can carry, derived from buildable
space rather than from villager count.
c91a398f1ab6:
At higher difficulties the cap also scales with villager count, so gatherers left
idle by an exhausted resource are absorbed into renewable sources instead of
standing around. Anchors extend to outposts and cities, so building continues at
the expansions once home is full.
d5dff2442e8b:
Actively trading for what the military needs. Gold used to pile up past 3000
while stone sat at 150 -- never quite enough for a castle -- because natural stone
and iron are far away and slow to gather.
So stone for a castle and iron for catapults count as military target resources:
they are not sold off as surplus, and spare gold buys them in, one lot at a time,
bounded by the target amount and a price ceiling.
ee41ab86cc37:
Military target resources are protected only up to the target amount; anything
beyond that is genuine surplus and may be sold, so a real glut is not hoarded.
ac501203a89f:
Research hunger. Under sustained fighting, military spending strips wood and gold
every few seconds, so opportunistic research never gets funded and unit unlocks
like siege and healing never happen in a real game.
The fix is a single event-triggered promotion: when the military brain wants a
unit whose unlock is missing, that research (and one missing prerequisite at a
time) is promoted to an income-fed bucket. At most one bucket exists at a time --
the unbounded version of this made the AI markedly worse.
7ea75729b8c9:
Research order: economic core first, then the military line (ranged attack,
cavalry, siege, attack/defence upgrades, medics), with the naval line inserted
only once there is a dock. Personality and counter picks are pushed to the front;
anything whose prerequisites are unmet is skipped naturally.
be6d5a970298:
Counter-pivot: once the opponent's civilisation has been identified, research
leans towards the line that counters it.
This looks like a bug and measurably is not: with the civilisation read disabled,
the hardest AI's win rate fell from 40% to 25%. An earlier attempt to move the
counter line after the economic core, based on a smaller sample, made things
worse and was reverted -- read the sample sizes before touching it again.
aaeceaee19f9:
Enemies at the door means fight first, do not build through it: construction
villagers die and new buildings block your own movement.
46bd93c11831:
A monastery is wanted for either of two independent reasons (same key, same
score, so it enters the pool once): it satisfies the building requirement for
advancing an age, and it is the entire bottleneck for medics -- the medic unlock
has no prerequisites and sits at the monastery, so "research it earlier" was
never the answer; having somewhere to research it was.
aff669ee0329:
Expansion sites: bare ore outside the economic radius that is reachable and
closer to us than to the enemy, with an outpost next to it to push the radius out.
The trigger is graded by difficulty, from passive (only once the home radius is
exhausted) through reactive and proactive to contested, where sites nearer the
enemy are acceptable.
d34b4f65587a:
============ 9c. AI fortifications: towers, walls and gates, castles ============
Before this the AI built no defences at all -- not broken, simply absent from its
build list.
Everything here is reactive, because a player who follows the rules does not tower
rush: towers go at the home approach, at a resource area raided twice, or beside
a new outpost; walls and a gate go up at the nearest chokepoint on our side only
after a full minute of cumulative defending; castles need stone that is genuinely
spare. Every placement goes through the player command path.
7c7d001fbc5e:
A gate is two tiles and its footprint always grows towards +x/+y, so a
chokepoint tile right at the edge of a gap puts the second tile into a wall or a
cliff. The anchor therefore tries the chokepoint tile and one tile back -- a
two-tile gap is exactly wide enough to take a gate.
14edfdd9f623:
Walls and gates are personality-driven: a rushing civilisation never builds them,
a turtling one builds pre-emptively, and everything in between waits until it has
spent enough time on the defensive. Only the nearest chokepoint on our own side
is closed, and the gate stays on the main road.
3b34618465ee:
============ 9d. AI navy: using the water on water maps ============
Map annotation runs once per map (water regions, fishery zones, buildable shore,
islands needing transport); the economy branch builds docks, fishing boats and
traps; the military branch runs the fleet as a group with its own
rock-paper-scissors composition, escorting fishing boats, blockading fords,
shelling the shore and retreating to port; and transports contest islands.
ea1ee06f1482:
Water connectivity, mirroring the land version. Shallows are navigable -- they
are a strait, not a wall -- and the regions define fishery zones and where a
fleet can operate.
14943347b053:
Fishery zones: sea-food nodes clustered by water region, the centroid serving as
a patrol and escort anchor.
5ba5d413194a:
Land regions holding bare ore are expansion targets. "Island" simply means a
region other than the one our own hub is in, evaluated per side at query time.
252be06fe390:
Training fishing boats goes through the command whitelist like everything else:
the zone must have fish and be under its cap.
c367f48d319d:
Individual ship movement -- the equivalent of the player dragging one ship, and
the only way the AI is allowed to move one. Writing coordinates directly is not
permitted.
1b1fe2b0be22:
Naval combat triangle: fire ships beat galleys, galleys beat siege ships, siege
ships beat fire ships. The fleet is one group.
9afa33c0d47a:
Enemy ship sightings, with the same slow-decaying memory as the land army and
through the same vision gate -- the AI never peeks.
c3b87fe7f413:
Retreat test, same as the land army and read from the difficulty knobs: if the
enemy-to-friendly ratio within 380px of the centroid passes the threshold, pull
back to the dock, where ships repair.
